game_name: Street Fighter II: The World Warrior

danger_signals.visual_form: health bar reduction (visual meter above character)
danger_signals.meaning: active threat level decreasing; opponent approaching defeat

danger_signals.visual_form: character stance change, visual effect burst, screen flash
danger_signals.meaning: high-damage attack incoming; immediate danger window

danger_signals.visual_form: countdown clock display in match UI
danger_signals.meaning: temporal threat; match outcome hanging in balance when time expires

danger_signals.visual_form: character stance shift, visual guard animation
danger_signals.meaning: opponent defensive posture; reduced damage vulnerability

safety_signals.visual_form: character spacing on screen, walking backward animation
safety_signals.meaning: player creating attack reaction time; reducing immediate threat

safety_signals.visual_form: blocking animation, guard sparks visual effect
safety_signals.meaning: damage negated; temporary safety window restored

safety_signals.visual_form: opponent knockdown animation, recovery frames visible
safety_signals.meaning: safe opening for offensive action; reduced incoming threat

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: character-specific fighting style and visual presentation
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: all characters are tournament competitors; no inherent faction enemies (mechanical system neutral)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: world map stage locations tied to character origin countries (Ryu-Japan, Guile-USA, Dhalsim-India, etc.)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: M. Bison/Vega visual design (final boss aesthetic distinction)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Bison design carries authority/threat visuals; Vega/Balrog as subordinate henchmen
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: narrative-only; no explicit stage environmental faction markers in tournament structure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: world map display showing progression through regional stages; character portraits appear as next opponents
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player advances through stages sequentially; defeated opponents are replaced on map

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: stage-specific background imagery (Brazil jungle for Blanka, Japan temple for E. Honda, Russia frozen landscape for Zangief); opponent character model displays with idle stance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: opponent AI executes fighting patterns; health bars present and active

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: knockout animation; opponent falls to ground or stumbles; stage returns to idle state
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: opponent ceases action; next map selection screen appears

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: scene transitions to unique environment (car lot, barrel yard, oil drum facility); destructible objects render as targets
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player performs attack combo against static environment; scoring mechanic replaces opponent combat

environmental_storytelling.example: E. Honda fights in traditional Japanese sumo wrestling dojo setting; Dhalsim's stage displays yoga temple environment; Guile's stage shows USA military barracks aesthetic — each stage visually reinforces character national/cultural origin
crowd visible in background of each stage; reactive crowd animations (cheering, reacting to knockdowns) creates sense of tournament legitimacy and social stakes
character-specific win poses and taunts communicate personality and fighting philosophy (Ryu meditative calm vs. Ken arrogant swagger) independent of narrative text
visual map progression from character's home country outward to global tournament venues implies increasing competitive prestige; final Bison stage positioned as narrative culmination

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: standing ready pose; meditative demeanor reflected in idle animation; communicates discipline and focus
confident smirk animation; more animated idle movements than Ryu; conveys arrogance and showmanship
wild-man posturing; erratic movement patterns; snarling animations communicate feral aggression
sumo wrestling crouch positioning; methodical stance communicates grounded, immovable quality
poised, martial arts formal stance; controlled animation conveys professional discipline
military-correct posture; rigid stance animations communicate military training and order
floating/levitation stance (visual); serene animation; fire breath effects communicate spiritual/mystical power
wrestler power stance; imposing physical presence; taunting animations communicate dominance
authoritative stance; aggressive animation patterns; psycho power visual effects (purple aura) communicate supernatural threat level above normal fighters
character pose shifts to guard animation; visual reduction in action range signals defensive mode

character performs unique special move animation sequence (Ryu Shoryuken rising uppercut, Guile Sonic Boom projectile, etc.); high-impact visual effects and sound design signal significant damage potential
character damage animation frequency increases; knockback response amplified; visual signaling of vulnerability

notes: Street Fighter II SNES operates primarily through direct mechanical manifestation rather than complex world-state systems. Visual communication centers on character individuality (fighting style, national origin, personality), match progression (health bars, round counters), and environmental stage identity. Faction systems are minimal — the game presents a tournament structure where all playable characters compete neutrally, with narrative faction (Shadolou) existing only in final-boss context with minimal visual distinction beyond character design. Regional differences in Japanese version include detailed character profiles with likes/dislikes and expanded win quotes that add personality context not visible in international builds. The game communicates danger primarily through health bar depletion and opponent animation states rather than environmental hazards or faction alignment mechanics.
